reviews.review_text:
Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin is a remastered version of the second game in the Dark Souls series. The game offers a familiar experience with only minor improvements compared to the previous game, and I felt there are a good number of drawbacks in game mechanics compared to DS 1 Remastered. While combat is simple to understand, it still requires attention, timing, and good positioning. The game continues to focus on thoughtful encounters rather than relying only on quick reactions.

The world design is one of the game’s strongest points. The areas are varied and detailed, and the environments encourage exploration. The remaster improves visuals, making lighting and textures clearer, which helps the world feel more alive. However, I felt DS1 level design felt better than DS2, especially in how the world connects and flows. Some sections are more crowded while others are emptier, changing how the player approaches challenges. Observation and patience are important throughout the journey.

Overall, Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin keeps the core identity of the original game, but the improvements feel limited with more drawbacks in game mechanics. The difficulty is still a major part of the experience, built into the entire game rather than just individual encounters. While it remains engaging and challenging, I personally felt DS1 Remastered was more refined in gameplay and world design.

Note:
1) I have attached a Steam guide for players using keyboard and mouse. I also played the game using keyboard and mouse controls.

2) For those buying Dark Souls 2: Scholar of the First Sin, it is better to get the vanilla version (DX9 bundle) and then purchase only the Scholar of the First Sin upgrade from its page. This can save some money instead of directly buying the full Scholar of the First Sin version. Both are the same game content-wise but the bundle plus upgrade method is slightly cheaper.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=1123186476

A continuation of the series that, despite not being made by the original author, actually feels even better in certain aspects. As a huge fan of Souls-like games, I got a lot of enjoyment out of playing it, even considering that I experienced it after Dark Souls 3 and Elden Ring.🌷
I definitely recommend it — it feels like a beautiful dark fairy tale set in the Dark Souls universe. The lore in this installment is somewhat detached from the main trilogy, but at least it’s more understandable than in the third game.

[h1] interesting experiment and design ideas, but tough to recommend as a first soulslike - if you liked DS1, you should at least give this a try [/h1]

[*] + weapon customisation, lore, powerstancing, lighting-based mechanics
[*] - some design choices that negatively affect player experience (generally, players don't want to feel frustrated while playing a game)

note: arachnophobia warning
[h1] n.b. I enjoyed my time with this game, but I cannot recommend it entirely because of its final vision and design; I only recommend this game because there is nothing suggesting that it is unplayable from a software perspective (i.e. crashing) [/h1]


[h2] pros [/h2]
[*] origin of some interesting ideas for soulslikes - bonfire ascetic to obtain NG+ state of certain areas before finishing the game, powerstancing with compatible weapons, infinite heal for more aggressive play, despawning enemies after killing them a certain amount of times
[*] good addition of lore that complimented the original game
[*] NG+ changes to enemy placement and positioning makes the game feel fresh on repeated playthroughs
[*] Torch mechanic increases the tension of a playthrough, where some areas are nearly entirely dark in the new DS2LE mod
[*] revamped weapon upgrade system compared to DS1 is now more forgiving for experimenting weapon elemental buffs (infusing and removing infusion)
[*] works the post-release DLC into the game in a thoughtful manner
[*] adjusted enemy placements and spawns compared to the original

[h2] cons [/h2]
[*] enemies don't feel fair due to a few mechanical changes - i) hyperarmor during attack animations means you cannot break poise to cancel an otherwise fatal attack - the game wants you to dodge or block that hit instead; ii) hitscans can generally be guessed correctly for smaller enemies, but some exceptions exist for larger enemies that have hitscans that are oversized compared to their attack animation; iii) enemies that are grouped together may have attack animations that almost perfectly overlap with their allies' downtimes, creating many standoffs that feel like time-wasters to test your patience
[*] a lot of bosses feel very similar to each other once you master their attack patterns - you can only fight so many humanoids with one-handed weapons (or so many dragons with a fire-breathing attack) before they become quite similar. that being said, bosses that allow 1v1 fights feel great to dodge and counter-attack
[*] the most common defense is that this game rewards patient gameplay to avoid creating "gank" situations where enemies swarm on you; but why are there so few tools for crowd control that you can easily access? spells are nerfed, few weapons are capable of sufficient DPS in an area of effect to clear enemies, enemies can't be easily stunned and have nearly unlimited access to any actions that require stamina. 
[*] you can accidentally add additional enemies to any encounter, with the frequency of NPC invasions (even while online) or the random number of magic horse-reindeer enemies in a DLC area; many of these additional enemies can be accidentally aggroed without any indication that you are about to do so
[*] certain levels are designed to punish quick runbacks to a boss fight (Iron Keep, Frigid Outskirts, No Man's Wharf, Lost Bastille, Black Gulch, Brightcove Tseldora, Huntsman's Copse) - rather than encourage a player to become better at the game, there is a risk of creating so much tedium from the time requirement for each retry that a player gives up instead of trying a different approach. in other areas, a shortcut exists for story progression which is helpful ([spoiler] Dragon Aerie [/spoiler], skipping the [spoiler] No Man's Wharf route to Lost Bastille [/spoiler])
[*] a lot of maps take place indoors, where it becomes really difficult to visualise where you need to go without the help of a general guide; this punishes players who are new to Soulslikes or on a blind playthrough
[*] losing a percentage of health when in Hollowed form after a death - it's not a new Soulslike mechanic, but the mechanic can backfire heavily from a design perspective in the early game which will discourage new players or those on a new playthrough
[*] DLC areas are interesting to explore, but reuse the giant Hollow Soldier design very frequently
[*] small player count on PC means that there is a strong chance you will be invaded by an experienced player; for the best experience, disconnect online functionality for your playthrough

[h2] other notes [/h2]
[*] DS2LE (DS2 Lighting Engine) mod with Path Tracing support revitalises the visuals of the original release, emphasizes the torch mechanic more heavily, 
and goes a long way in making the presentation much better
[*] https://xenevel.github.io/dark-souls-2-sotfs-cheat-sheet/ is a great resource for checking off achievement requirements for sorceries, hexes, miracles and pyromancies; some parts of this guide can be skipped entirely or modified once you know what you are doing, and some minor information is outdated (e.g. armor received from an NPC has previously been changed to an enemy drop)
[*] preferred build for this review was STR based, finishing the game with powerstanced +10 Giant Club (R Hand) and +10 Large Club (L Hand)
[*] Path Tracing mod may affect your framerate (and thus input latency) - depending on your settings, aim for >60fps to obtain the most responsiveness for your controls

So so much better than Demon's Souls. While the  bosses were insanely easy except for Red Smelter Demon, Fume Knight (goated), Darklurker, and maaaybe Velstadt. Everything was fun to dodge and not nearly as mindless as people give them credit for. Most bosses I beat in like 3 tries but they were still fun and enjoyable. The world design is.. weird. Kinda interconnected, kinda not, and the choice to have the Forest and Tower of Flame both lead to the same singular branching path dead end was an odd choice.

It looks great, plays great, gameplay feels less janky than fromsoft's previous two games, and in some ways it feels like it does some things even better than ds1. Weapons and armor rarely ever break cause they repair themselves at bonfires, flasks are nerfed but you have lifegems which are like demons souls grass heals, and runbacks... aren't bad at all! The only ones I struggled with at all was really just the old iron king, and that's only cause I didn't know about the secret bonfire right next to the arena until after the fight. Sir Alonne was 2 rooms and blue smelter was 3 rooms, like wtf how do people complain this much about these, ds1 was way way way worse (frigid outskirts sucked tho). People prolly run through the levels and have the entire area's enemy count chasing them to the fog gate then complain about "bad game", but you as a player should EASILY be able to tell when you can run past to the gate and when you should fight your way through one-by-one, it's communicated insanely well to the player and I think souls fans are just dummy dums.

Overall it felt like a streamlined, pretty darn fun and consistent souls game. One of the weaker entries sure, but gosh is it still good and worth a playthrough.

Base game: 8/10 (36hr 30m)
Sunken King DLC: 5/10 (3hr 37m)
Old Iron King DLC: 8/10 (3hr 51m)
Ivory King DLC: 6/10 (3hr 35m)

When Dark Souls 2 came out and I tried playing it, I was disappointed because it was completely different from what I’d experienced in the first game, and I said I’d never play it again and that the game was bad. But look—more than 10 years later, I gave it another chance. The improved edition of the game made me think it would definitely have been revamped in some way, and that’s exactly what happened. As much as I didn’t want to play DS2, I’m having so much fun, and I have to say it’s hated for no reason. Yes, it’s certainly not the best in the series, but so far I’m having a lot of fun discovering the story and interesting areas, and I know that dozens of hours still await me.

this is the best game ever made.
i got dropped on my head as a child and ever since then i have not been able to enjoy video games.
until this game came along.
thank you shinzo abe for taking one for the team.

Dark Souls II is one of the most unique games in the series. It has an amazing atmosphere, tons of build variety, and some of the best replay value in the Souls games. The world feels mysterious and lonely in a really memorable way, and the DLC areas are incredible. While it does a few things differently from the other games, that’s part of what makes it special. If you enjoy exploration, challenging combat, and experimenting with different weapons and builds, DS2 is absolutely worth playing.
Honestly not as bad as people say. Although the enemy placement in some parts are weird and some runbacks (Frigid Outskirts) are painful DS2 is overall a good experience.
This game is overhated. Yes, it has flaws, but it's still amazing. Highly recommend to people who enjoy soulsborne games and soulslikes.
During final boss fight I cried every time a new boss showed up. 10/10 would torture myself for this game again.

A very Critical and Controversial game for me and within the community. And i can definitely see why. Alot new and diffrent mechanics and combat System. But its still a Souls game and a part of the family and it for sure has its moments. If you are a Fan of Souls games then try this game too. You dont have to 100 percent it but at least try it. If you dont like it, then simply just move on. :)

I was really going into this one with good faith, but I have to say, there's really good reason this one is so hated and loved in the community. I say skip it, because I think it is a bad game. So much love has been put into it with the world details, art direction, lore that is actually a bit different and just vibes, and it is really painful to see it get dragged down by terrible hit boxes, adaptation (the stat), forgettable bosses, bad level design, losing your health on every death, run backs that go on forever and some of the most annoying sections in a video game. I know that this game had a rushed development, because overambition really took over and the devs lost time, but some of this also just seems like either laziness or incompetence, and I can just in good conscience recommend it as a game. It's a game with excellent wallpaper, just a rotten wall behind it though.
Overhated game. Besides the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ keybinds, this game is a solid entry in the Dark Souls trilogy.

most likely a skip. Just play all other from software games. And if you platinum all of them and really want something new then play this. Otherwise you wont be missing much that you can't just get from better souls games

It's baffling how this game achieved over a 90 metacritic when it is a massive downgrade from Dark Souls 1 in every conceivable way despite coming out several years afterward. The controls are imprecise and janky, which is inexcusable in a game that demands precision timing. A number of terrible design decisions were made in this game that were never made again in Dark Souls 3 for obvious reasons.

Not a worthy successor to Dark Souls 1, it feels like a low budget, half assed souls like copy rather than a sequel.

Dark Souls 2 is the black sheep of the Souls series. 

DS2 is such a weird "souls" game but it is still a good game, it feels different from the other souls game. Yes the gameplay can be little clunky and slow, the runback is atrocious, it has a lot of mobs and the bosses aren't that memorable. But it still feels like a such a special game despite all that. There is something quite magical in the "flaws". The areas and settings are fun to explore and there is a lot of variety. The DLCs also are quite good. 

In terms of difficulty, the game isn't that hard especially since the bosses are actually really easy, especially if you come from more modern fromsoft games where the bosses has more complex moveset. Dark Souls 2 bosses are so simple in terms of moveset and easy to predict. The struggle are more in the areas and runbacks.

The atmosphere in this game is almost as good as Dark Souls 1 IMO. Majula is the best hub of all video games. It is so peaceful and beautiful.

Despite the apparent flaws of the game, there is something magical in it i can't quite explain.

I can't speak about the other version, but this is by far the worst game out of the three. If you've played the other two AND demon souls AND elden ring AND bloodbourne and you want more, get this. But there are certain areas in this game that I can only conclude were only allowed into the final version through sheer negligence. Movement is clunky and unsatisfying, a good proportion of the bosses are underwhelming or just unfun. Besides powerstancing, which I barely used anyways, and Majula, there were just so few times when i had an actually satisfying experience. I don't get how you had dark souls II already, and instead of fine-tuning the worst aspects of, you manage to make it worse apparently.

Actually loved Dark Souls 2! I think it's over hated and I really liked the weirdness of this game from the music, to the animations, to the sound effects. Can be frustrating at times but that is a trademark of this series so I can't be too mad about it

An improvement on the original despite the adaptability stat and many bosses that are just another guy in armor.

The environments are fondly memorable. Majula is the best hub area in any of the Dark Souls games. Heide's Tower of Flame and the Shrine of Amana are gorgeous. The Undead Purgatory and the pit are such good conceptual ideas from a dark fantasy story/world building perspective. 

The story is arguably the best in the series for bridging the overarching lore with a real plot. [spoiler] Nashandra makes for a great villain. Loved her entrance through the fog with the player as her final obstacle. [/spoiler]

Losing maximum health feels terrible at first, but there are plenty of items to restore it. You can also help other players with levels/bosses to restore yourself, which provides a meaningful reward for cooperative play.
its dark souls, and still an amazing experience. don'st listen to the ngeative ppeople saying to skip this one, its worth it.
DS2 Has a beautiful world and an amazing feels to the game one of my faivourate games ever

If someone tells you to skip this game after playing the first, don't listen to them. They are bad at the game. 

I do have criticisms. Some boss fights are complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, and the hit detection is broken. You will be hit by attacks that don't even touch you. I get that they wanted to make a more extreme Dark Souls, and I'm all for that, but the death mechanic is too punishing. That, paired with the unbalanced jank, can make a man go mad...

don't give up, skeleton.

I would recommend trying DS2 after DS1(and maybe also after DS3) but be mindful that this game is quite different, probably better to be aware of these things going in, such as how dodge rolls are affected by a stat, and how there are some jank with how enemies swing their weapons, and surely something else I have forgot about by now...

SotFS is probably the best version to play, but both versions should be 60 fps, there are also some positives and negatives of both the new and old versions, so either way you find something to complain about surely.

In the end I did enjoy it but you really have to get into and understand how this game is different, and it might hurt getting there.

Will update the review when I finish the game, but a dozen hours into my current playthrough and I got to say that while yes there is a ♥♥♥♥-ton of enemies scattered around, the world doesn't make sense therefore confuses people looking for DS1 exploration, and some the bosses can just be base enemies beefed up with attack, health, and or numbers, I think this game is misunderstood by some of the souls community, IT'S STILL A GOOD GAME! It is the most experimental title in the series, it certainly has a bit of a learning curve coming from DS1, but I think that is what makes some fall in love with this game, it really isn't just Dark Souls but the 2nd time, it makes sweeping changes in some parts and little changes in other parts. I like the preservation of maintaining a high level of challenge (at least initially) that Dark Souls is known for, this game forces you to play a bit differently than the other DS games, and that is refreshing after dumping over 40 hours in DS1. So far so good.

This remains my favorite Souls game to this day, I absolutely love it. I understand all the complaints about it and don't really disagree with them. There is definitely jank, there are definitely some wild decisions, and many moments where the game feels genuinely unfair and poorly designed. There were weird changes and plans with the development of the game. However, the mechanics are interesting to me, and I love the world and characters. The impact this game had on the design of the future ones is undeniable, and the game truly feels like it has heart. I genuinely get happy and excited talking about this game.
After waiting for years to get the game, I can honestly say this game rocks. Ds3 is better for sure, and so is ER. But dang I am having a blast so far.

it's better than the critics make it out to be

The most annoying thing is the quasi-instant equip break effects

First from software game I ever played, in addition to being my introduction to souls-like games. I chose this game as my first blindly based off the part of the community that hate the game more than anything else on the planet, I did really struggle at the start because I was still understanding hit boxes, however once I leveled up enough to be able to make use of I-frames, the game became easier, I will admit that I probably wouldnt have 100% of the achivements if it wasnt for a checklist guide I found on reddit, but I still really enjoyed this game, additionally played with no spoilers, with the only content I had seen was whats on the store page.

- There is a small issue with starting a new playthrough on controller, as when you go to enter your name it sometimes just wipes what you entered as opposed to confirming, but using keyboard and mouse for that part completely fixed that issue, the invaders i met in the game were surprisngly respectful and would wait until I was actually ready to fight them as opposed to just one tapping players immediately like i saw online.

First Dark Souls I've ever played, it was hard and annoying and I had to watch a youtuber doing everything while I was trying to follow him for items you can easily miss, and there is a whole lot of them! I hear bad things internet schizos say about this game, but I liked walking in the cursed swamps so much! More info go search for it.
the most nasty and evil fromsoft game. must play if you're a fan of the genre, but trust nobody who has this as their favorite one

Hated it first time I played and didn't play for months got bored and came back and actually learned the mechanics and its not entirely bad because the actual bosses are easy its the mobs that ruin it for me but still at least they disappear if they become a problem.

The next souls/blood/elden (exclude Sekiro) should actually use a lot that's in this game like the poison and toxic actually being useful and certain "boss items" should just become obtainable in future games with different name maybe super cool items in this game.

Don't buy if your first Souls game start with DS1 than do DS3 than Elden than this and the others don't buy Sekiro first lol please that game is for masters only.

This just pales in comparison to the other souls titles. They introduced so many weird design decisions that made it unenjoyable for me and I have no desire to ever replay this one. Thankfully they managed to turn it around again with Dark Souls 3.

Worst game in trilogy BY FAR , hate that it gets is DESERVED and NOT ENOUGH. Playing for SOLO reason to finish all FromSoftware games , if ur NOT playing for completionism then i ADVISE YOU to just skip this failure of game.

Noting works beside booting up this pile of dogsht.

Peak souls 2... there is a reason people love & hate this title. Personally, I'm on the former camp. This game has some unique ideas that we do not see on the rest of Dark Souls franchise, it just has this flavor that sets it apart. It will test your patience at times, so take your time and do not underestimate anything especially on your first playthrough. The variety of scenery is insane and it's absolutely gorgeous, especially on late game.
art design good gameplay bad. only people who disagree use the rapier and warped sword or magic

DS2 is honestly overhated. It has just as much ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ as the first game, with some innovations in bullshittery of course. It is a sequel after all.

Runbacks are still harder than the bosses. Quests and secrets are just as obscure. The enemies are more aggressive, but in return the player is a bit more agile. In some areas you WILL get jumped (especially the DLCs); they seem built for cooperative play or summoning NPC phantoms. It's all doable, although you will likely get frustrated at one point or another.

Still, this game is at lot easier than I remember it being when I played it as a child. If you've finished ER you can beat this game.
DS1  and DS3 are better. This game is good though. Just remember you get swarmed more, and gotta grind a little more then the other darksouls, or elden ring

Movement and combat feel like ♥♥♥♥ compared to DS1 and Elden Ring. After recently finishing DS1 i was really excited for the second game. And wow its just not as good. I have no interest in continuing this game and i loved playing through DS1 and Elden Ring. Might just skip this one and go to DS3 instead.

HELL YEAH, the best dark souls (for me) in terms of atmosphere and gameplay ideas + literally easiest of the series so the chillest one too. I'd give it a solid 8/10. Pretty ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up stage of a game - Iron Keep that's obvious. Hurrah for the platinum!

Wouldn’t recommend this over the rest of the franchise, try ds1, 3 or elden ring then consider coming back.

The game is definitely weird compared to the rest, even Ds1 has better movement which I found to be odd since it’s older. 

The main problem I have with this game is the fact I just can’t use a keyboard and mouse which I prefer over controller, for some reason my left click just doesn’t work most of the time, my mouse isn’t broken since it works fine on everything else, it’s only in ds2, i had to switch to my Xbox controller just to play the game properly which is unfortunate.
Its a good game takes a while to get used to the controls. not as good as the other games but its still good
It's great for what it is. It's still apart of the souls trilogy, but i feel like it should've been its own stand alone game. Like Sekiro or Bloodborne.

Best souls game.
Spiritual prequel to Elden Ring.
The haters should have leveled adaptability irl.
I loved this game overall - apart from a few ganky areas and some underwhelming bosses it is peak souls.

Rough in places but overall really solid, it definitely holds up better than the first one in my opinion. don't make the mistake of trying to play with a mouse and keyboard, I switched over to steam deck about 10 hours in and had a much better time

The hate this game receives is nothing more than a bandwagon. There’s so much good about this game. After putting some time into this title, I’ve come to the realization that people will literally spit on anything.

Don’t listen to the hate. Deep down, people dislike this one because it wasn’t made directly by Miyazaki, but they’ll never say it out loud because it would destroy their argument that the game is poorly designed.

After leveling my ADP to 32, it was smooth sailing. Dark Souls 2 has so much charm, and Majula is the best hub area in the entire franchise.

The starting controls was pretty mid had to change a bunch of keybinds and the start of the game isnt the most friendly compared to the first dark souls but so far its been a blast 7-8 out of 10 so far recomended
Atmosphere of the game did most of the carrying. Game is mostly annoying and unfair. Still if you have played Souls-Like games, this one is still worth a try.

This game blows, such a step down from Dark Souls 1

EDIT: Okay the game is fun now 

EDIT 2: Iron King and Ivory King DLC were FIRE. Sunken King you STINK
Def a learning curve compared to other souls games but good

Muy buen juego con sus detalles claro pero no por eso incompleto o roto si te gustan los desafios cumplirá tus expectativas de sobra 9/10 le doy después de 580 hrs de juego y si es el mas dificil de los 3 souls

If you came from DS1, DS3, Sekiro, Bloodborne, Elden Ring, this game is not it. I'm sure there are people here who have experienced this game and enjoyed it completely but i played for 10 hours, got my dodge iframes to feel right via leveling ADP, beat the first 2 bosses and i cant keep going. Play any of those other FromSoftware games if you want to enjoy yourself cause this is certainly the black sheep of the family.  Boss fights were boring, literally parried the first one the entire fight, second one was also not better. The level design with enemy placement and density is atrocious, difficulty comes in the form of many smaller enemies bum rushing you and chasing you throughout the entire level, one hit you get staggered and then proceed to get stun locked by everything else. Also beware of ledges, you can accidently heavy attack yourself to death, a lot. I never played DS2 when it came out so i have no nostalgia for it, If this is you id recommend maybe trying out another title like those mentioned or one of the indie souls-likes. I do give the game credit because i believe through making this game and getting all the feedback Fromsoftware came back better and took a lot of those lessons into their better titles.

At first I didn't like the game because controls are configured by default to make it unplayable with keyboard/mouse. 

But I found it was possible to do this:
- use Q and E for confirm and back (no need to use a mouse click or a context menu)
- set up right click and left click for left and right attack (need to reverse it for some reason)
- set strong attack to CTRL
- most important, to enable/disable double click after EACH game launch to avoid attack delays

and after this the game started to work. It is amazing. Really good Dark Souls experience with a lot of yet unknown maps, unique covenants, unique rings, and still working PvP.

While there are certainly some quirks, and I probably wouldn't recommend it as a first souls game, it is a very solid and underrated entry in the franchise, and I am enjoying my time with it.

Be aware to dodge effectively you will need to level Adaptability which boosts Agility, but this game throws levels at you very quickly ane getting a few extra levels is very easy, you actually have a ton of choices and flexibility in this game as a result.

I also would not recommend this version (Scholar of the First Sin) for a first playthrough. The mobs and enemy counts at times feel like trolling, resulting in some of the worst runbacks of all time. However this game also had a unique feature where after a certain number of kills (12) they stop respawning, so do expect some grindy areas. 60 fps is nice (this remaster vs 30 in original) but this game has slow and methodical combat this is not a twitch game. If you are looking for very fast aggressive combat this isn't it.

The game is huge, offers tons of variety in builds, loot, scenery, has some awesome levels (and a few frustrating clunkers). Would give a strong recommend to anyone who enjoys souls games.

Dark Souls II’s legacy precedes it. Everywhere on the internet, someone has something to say about it. It’s certainly the most polarizing Soulsborne game, unlike its universally beloved predecessor. Why? Why do people treat it so much differently than Fromsoft’s other Soulsborne titles? I’ll answer that in this review.
This review's gonna be a little different. I’m going to list changes made from Dark Souls I in order for you to properly understand how it’s different. I’m choosing Dark Souls I because 1. It came right before this game, 2. It set the standard for the Soulsborne games going forward, and 3. You should have played it if you’re here. If you haven’t, play the first game before looking at this one.
These changes will be in no particular order.

Positives
-Faster gameplay in general. Movement is faster, enemies are faster, weapons are, everything is.
-Estus flask upgrades are separated between ones that increase the number of Estuses you get when recovering from a bonfire, and ones that increase the amount each Estus heals.
-You can warp from any bonfire to any bonfire from the start of the game.
-The game does a better job overall at explaining stuff
-4 ring slots instead of 2
-Cursed affect is nerfed
-You can respec your stats (change them) whenever you want, and customize your character after character creation.
-New Game + adds new things to levels, i.e bosses appearing in different spots
-Bosses are better in general
-New item called Bonfire Ascetic lets you completely reset an area, enemies and all
-Quick climbing up ladders. (Essentially if you hold sprint you can climb faster up them)
-Stat multiaffects (when you upgrade some stats, others get small upgrades too)
-Life gems (smaller healing items that are used like regular items as opposed to Estuses. Really helpful in the early game)
-NPCs don’t attack back if you attack them, and remain friendly
-More customization and accessibility settings
-Weapon durability is restored whenever you visit a bonfire
-Lighting is really good


Negatives
	-Torch mechanic (you can’t see well in many levels without it, somewhat similar to the lantern in the Tomb of the Giants from DS1 but it’s on a timer)
	-When you die, the game caps your health. (This increases the more you die by 5% each time, and it bottoms at 50%. You can reverse this by using a human effigy, which is the Humanity of this game. There is also a ring that reduces this bottom to 75%.)
	-Positioning-based damage debuffs (Weapons have strong spots and weak spots, and if your weapon clips through the environment, it does less damage to enemies. This also counts with hitting multiple enemies)
	-Dodgeroll invincibility frames are tied to a stat, Adaptability (you get more iframes the more you upgrade it)
	-Equip load now has its own stat, Vitality (equip load is what you can carry onto you)
	-Stamina is massively nerfed (dodging and attacking burns way too much stamina)
	-Movement system is locked to 8 cardinal directions (essentially, you can only move North, East, South, West, and the inbetweens for those. Not quite as bad as you think but still bad)
	-Enemies follow you until thresholds set by the devs, not by the enemy ai
	-Animations such as opening doors or going through fog gates don’t have iframes
	-Regular-ass doors close every time you die
	-Level design in general is a lot worse than DS1
	-Game doesn’t direct you to the right areas very well (oftentimes you won’t know where to go next because you keep ending up in areas with enemies that will 1-shot you)
	-Every area forces you to warp back to the hub instead of circling back to it like in DS1
	-Healing from Estus Flasks is super slow
	-Hit box is constantly scummed (you’ll end up getting hit by things you feel like you should not have been hit by)
	-Weapon durability drains super fast
	-Enemies respawn a limited amount of time (could be seen as a positive? If you join a certain covenant they respawn infinitely)
	-You can’t level up at bonfires for some reason? You have to warp back to the hub and talk with an NPC


I know that was a lot of negative stuff. Do I think the game is good? Yes, I do. It’s not quite on the same level as many of the other Soulsborne games, and mechanically shares more with Demon’s Souls than Dark Souls. However, it still is an important member of the Dark Souls family. 
Despite what people would tell you, this game is worth it if you truly enjoyed Dark Souls 1. Don’t listen to the people who tell you to skip this one, or play it last. The order you should play the Dark Souls games in is the one that they give you; 1, 2, 3. All of them are vital cogs in the machine that is the Dark Souls trilogy.

This video helped me a lot with writing this review. It’s also very good and funny.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=omvwUNdtwa4&list=PLfN_wAPkmk2hp5VgThI9gw9pK-v-Hlg79&index=81

I was originally scared I wouldn't like this game and waste my money, but after playing through it, it was some of the most fun I've had in a while. Yes there are some weird things and the game has some issues, but that comes with it's age, and I think that's part of it's charm. 

I honestly wasn't ready to enjoy the adaptability stat, like what do you mean I have to level a stat to get better i-frames? However, I actually found that this allowed for more build diversity, and it added some challenge that wasn't there in the first Dark Souls. It makes you choose a priority and forces you to "git gud" with whatever playstyle you choose. There's also the issue that is quickly apparent. The loss of max HP on death up to 50%. This is troubling as a new player, but again isn't all that bad. Most enemies in the game aren't that difficult to defeat, and even many bosses are not that bad. With many weapons I felt weirdly powerful, chunking everything with each hit. Upgrading your weapon occasionally feels difficult, but if you're willing to farm for the souls or explore areas that you might be under leveled for, it's entirely doable.

There was and probably still is a lot of hate for this game online, and I feel like it's honestly one of those hidden gems that the masses are finally seeing in a different light (myself included). This game definitely grew on me, going from a game I had an irrational fear of to my favorite of the entire Soulsborne series. I would highly recommend this to those that played a Souls game or two and want something unique and interesting, or even to those that are veterans of the Souls series that are looking for something different than the others. Even if the story is in a land far from Lordran and doesn't seem connected, there are so many connections that I'm still finding more all the time.

Dark Souls 2.
TLDR: I love this game. I hate this game. 
This game is a enjoyable, puzzle solving experience. This game is a frustrating collection of poorly curated game mechanics. The mechanics are so intricate and complex! The story is such an unmitigated mess!
I've heard a lot about this game since I picked it up. All of those things I listed above are... statements I've seen made about it by members of the community. Having played so much of it already, (though without having finished,) I can confirm that I agree with all of those statements listed above.
I love this game. I hate this game.
Dark Souls 2 is... a diamond in the rough. Very rough, at times, but very pretty at others. And that is the biggest thing about this game that keeps me from being willing to unequivocally recommend it, though I do, indeed recommend it. There are just... several things that anyone interested in picking up this game needs to understand.
1. The jank: Yeah, the jank is sure there. Some of it is kind of neat (see some of the glitches this game has on offer), kind of endearing (it feels like an older game, and I enjoy that), but a lot of it is just frustrating. A LOT of enemies have unreasonably large hitboxes for certain attacks. Your player character has, on occasion, an unreasonably large hurtbox for receiving those attacks. I've seen a recording of someone, on the present patch, getting killed by an arrow that "hit" about a body's width away from the avatar.
2. The gank. Yep, for those who haven't heard, this is THE souls-like gank game. Area upon area exist where you'll be swarmed by high-health, high-damage enemies, especially if you rush through. It wasn't often an enormous issue for me, since I tend to be willing to patiently slog through the area, confident I'll get a checkpoint at a bonfire soon enough, but even so, I found several areas with no choice but to fight a mob, all of whom were capable of killing me one on one. So keep in mind, those of you who want to just blast on through every region, that's almost never an option. At least on the first engagement with any given area. Once doors are open and levers are pulled, one often can do that running through. Its the areas where running through is NEVER feasible that the game grows tiring.
3. The age. This game shows its age. That is partly a good thing - it rarely had performance problems on my mid-tier PC. But that's also a bad thing - I did occasionally have performance issues. In a twelve year old game, on what is right now a middle-tier rig. It was mostly the odd experience of it lagging right before loading a sound, which is especially disconcerting in a place full of enemies. The age is also made obvious in the environment. It looks good enough, but the walls and floors often have notably larger hitboxes than their textures would imply. This makes sniping things with arrows into a guessing game. Will your projectile collide with the mass of apparently solid air that lines every surface in the game, or will it will fly toward its intended destination?
But that list of issues is only part of my opinion on this game, because do recall:
I love this game. I hate this game.
I do, in fact, love aspects of this game. The environments are (mostly) really cool. Everything is certainly atmospheric. Each region feels unique and distinct, and I really like that. I think that how they're stitched together is sometimes really weird, but that's no deal breaker in my book.
The enemy designs are typically quite neat. A few feel uninspired, and a few have attacks that are just ridiculous for this kind of game (and that is definitely part of jank) but most fit their environment, and several are just really cool, like the encounter with the Burnt Ivory King and Aava. Several are also absolutely terrible - looking at you, Imperfect and Lost Sinner. And some... are certainly there. Like the Rotten, and the Old Iron King.
Yes, I did just praise Aava. I know lots of people who've played this game don't like her, but I do. And I'd also like to put it out there that I think that Lud and Zallen are a cool encounter too, okay? Their hitboxes might be... generous, but once you know how to dodge them, their fights becomes engaging. They hit hard, but you have to figure out how not to get hit, and find your windows for hitting them in exchange. They feel like raid bosses if you bring NPC summons with you; they feel like challenging obstacles if you fight them alone.
Honestly, I enjoy this game the most when it gives you options on how to deal with things. Summon or don't? Dodge an attack, or block it? If you do dodge, do you go to one side, away from the enemy, or toward? Should you go around behind, or will the enemy have an answer to that? (Spoiler, many enemies don't, but they can often turn quickly.) This makes the game feel like a puzzle to be solved by the player, who must pay attention to tease out the answer. That, I feel, is this game at its peak.
In the same vein of thought, that is also when the game is at its most frustrating - it frequently takes that wealth of options away. The ganking is the worst offender, by far. Nothing is more powerful than being ganged up on by the denizens of the Iron Keep or the Iron Passage or the Old Bastille. Get hit once, then get stunlocked and become unable to do anything. Bring spells? Unless you cast them before you enter gank town, you're out of luck, buddy. Weapons and shields? You might have enough stamina to block and dodge, but sometimes you run out of stamina before you can attack, or you can't get any attacks off without getting got. Hit and runs? While typically available in the base game, this is simply not an option in some spots in the DLCs. This feeling of brittleness, of not having any flexibility in your choices (for viable options, at least), of just having to bang your head against a brick wall just to get a barely workable solution through RNG, only to know you may have to do it again repeatedly if your own abilities fail you or you get hit by a wayward attack and die - that is probably the worst aspect of this game. Ugh, the Executioner's Chariot fight was totally an example of that.
Skill issue? Maybe. Game issue? Totally.
At the end of the day, if you've got a hundred hours that you're willing to put into a single playthrough of a game, you should probably try Dark Souls 2. The puzzle solving is fun, and I've totally gotten my money's worth out of it, for that alone. If you feel you don't have a hundred hours, you could still pick it up - I've been playing this game in small bursts of a few hours at a time, mostly after work, and its pretty easy to put down and pick up again. If you want a smaller time commitment but still want to try a Souls-like, play Dark Souls one. Still challenging, still rewarding, but a shorter game. And if you want a Souls-like with less jank... I would still recommend you try the prior game, Dark Souls. But... be forewarned... I understand all of these FromSoft games have their own rough edges, and I know DS1 is no exception.
So, if you need a summary,
Some times, I love this game, and some times, I hate this game. But I still like it.

I know that Dark Souls 2 is widely considered the worst in the franchise, and while yes I do have issues with the game, to call it the *worst* is pure nonsense. I would honestly much rather play DS2 over DS1 any day of the week, and that includes the remaster they came out with shortly after DS2 came out. 

Now, will I say that Dark Souls 2 is flawless? Absolutely not. Dark Souls 2 has plenty of issues. Some of the hitboxes are kind of weird, but that only happens if you do not level the adapability stat. The only exception I can think of to where that doesn't happen is with Fume Knight, and he's already ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ as is. but if you have the Scholar of The First Sin edition (the version I played) a lot of the issues are kinda just... gone. 

It took me 31 hours to beat the game, mostly because of Fume Knight eating up most of my boss attempts while going through the game. If you like the Soulslike genre, definitely play it. If this is your first Souls game... well then that's a different story. Definetly play the other games first BEFORE coming to this, because if you do start off with this game, you are going to hate it. 

Dark Souls 2 was the last game in my journey from playing all the souls games. It ranks up there in my top three, with Bloodborne in first, Dark Souls 2 in second, and Elden Ring and Dark Souls 3 being interchangeable.

first of all yes this is a very diffrent game compared to ds1 and ds3, but dont let that stop you . the story in this game is actually good, the combat is similar yet diffrent, yes hit boxs are janky in this game but with time you and "adapt" to this style of gameplay, only issue is that theres alot of enemies in the scholar of the first sin edition compared to base game, so take your time in clearing them out, just give it a chance its not as bad as people say it is

Don't believe the people saying DS2 is bad.
It is the worst of the 3. Has it's issues, but not bad.

Changes of how healing works, damage numbers and some new mechanics and a few other things but still a great game. And still a good Dark Souls game. Maybe it's the least popular, because some changes are a bit "underwhelming" or questionable but I still enjoyed it. I love it.
I love all 3 Dark Souls games and all 3 does some things differently than the rest. 
They are "variations for a theme". But still have their own charm. 
Liking or not liking this game is mostly a matter or personal taste and tolerance for some annoying design decisions.

this is probably the jankiest of the trilogy, however it has a lot of charm. take this game at a much slower pace and I think most Dark Souls fans will come to like it despite the controversial opinions on it.

The most varied, innovative and clever dark souls game. It has cool mechanics that didn't continue with later games, plus awesome build variety. It also has some weird and unwelcome mechanics that can trip you up if you expect things to work the same as the other Dark Souls titles. Overall, I think it has a ton of charm, and is well worth playing.

This game doesn't play like Dark Souls 1, and you shouldn't expect it to. It can be difficult to pick up at first but once you can get through learning a few different mechanics this game really becomes one of the best feeling games out there. It will always come second fiddle (Or in this third fiddle) to 1 and 3 but that doesn't make this game bad by any means. It has some awesome boss designs, awesome levels and probably the best PVP out of the three. I highly recommend this game just make sure you take your time!

I think it's an interesting example of many weird trends, peculiarities, eccentricities, and what-have-you that even though the game ends up being probably the weakest in the series it's more fascinating to dissect than DS3, and probably even Elden Ring. Just really pick apart the minutia that led to this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Some of these questions and moments are ingrained into my memory, I'll be thinking about this stupid ♥♥♥♥ until the day I die.

Remember randomly fighting Shadow Ornstein? How Soul Memory being an anti-twink mechanic ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up literally everything multiplayer related? Remember how they built up the Pursuer? What about when they talked about how they wanted to punish backstab fishing so the turtle knights fall on you and then it's forgotten? What about that impressive ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ lighting that looked badass and how they wanted to emphasize torches and stealth? Or the intro video that has zilch to do with the plot? Or that the plot is so absolutely all over the place even Dark Souls lore schizos throw up their hands and say it's convoluted? Oh, what about the Mirror Knight in that same pre-rendered footage looking ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ awesome in the rain and lightning with an actual mirror look to him as he summoned enemy phantoms? The Sunken King destroying all weaponry in like 4 hits because the PC frame rate makes everything break twice as fast on top of his innate corrosion? The weird obsession with the box things that Elden Ring would kind of do with the stone sword keys? Or the forest full of invisible enemies. The guy whose sole job is to build ladders, because these games are obsessed with ladders? What about how every single area in this game is either literal hell like the Gutter or the Shrine of Rapidfire Rocket Guided Magic Missiles or like two feet long and then you're already in a new area? And surely you recall that for some reason enemies only respawn a certain amount of times. Why? And those NG+ invaders. The complaints about ambushes then Scholar giving MORE ambushes. Hell, just the fact that this was essentially rereleased just to try and fix fan reception. The hardmode covenant being right next to the starter guide NPC. The Reindeer Ride to Hell. This game is probably even where it became enshrined in some people that From just kind of does the same things over and over, and boy does it like to retread territory. And as I begin to mention the terrible dueling covenant, the hospice nurse does the world a favor and smothers me.

Time spent on the clock writing this review: 32 min

Get the base game, get some 4k texture upgrades and the lighting mod and it does a lot to help the game look modern. Great fun, lots of dying. Super deep lore, environmental storytelling, and lots of ways to build your character.

Definitely the weakest modern From Software game but there's a reason some people have another name for Elden Ring, which Dark Souls 2-2.

Personally my favorite Souls-like, the only reason DS3 has more playtime in my library is because I have also played the regular DS2 in pc and Xbox. 

That said, it is objectively speaking filled with issues, most of which comes from its troubled development cycle while some of those are just dumb choices that we the player have to overcome.

A substat that affects how many i-frames my dodge roll has? And increasing said substat can be done by leveling up two separate main stats? Thankfully there's more than enough souls (currencry) to just dump enough into ADP (Adaptability) as the first stat to get enough I-frames to match DS1 and DS3. Sadly the only way to know how much to level up ADP (and/or attunement if you're a caster) is looking that up outside of the game.

If there's one thing DS2 does the best it's New Game+, with plenty of changes to surprise and frighten you. 

I recommend this game for any souls-like enjoyer but with the caveat that either you're gonna need to look up plenty of stuff (such as manual aiming during lock-on while using great hammers, great axes and ultra greatswords) or you're just gonna have to power through and do your own in-game reseach like I did when DS2 first launched on Xbox.

The consequences of letting the armor core team do dark souls is evident by the game being surface level about dark souls features but missing the depth you appreciate in 1 and 3. Unpopular opinion, but I think this game is better than Dark Souls 1 Remastered. It has better combat, less clunky mechanics, better bosses, better enemies, and simpler inventory and item functionality. That being said, there are some parts that are worse. The story/lore, characters, and map are much less memorable, and the mechanic of losing max HP each death is more annoying than interesting. The DLCs were good, and better than DS1's DLCs. Achievements were a grind because the game is so long, and some are tied to covenant rank, which means you must play online or farm drops with low drop rates. Good game overall. 8.2/10
I have some gripes compared to vanilla ds2, but it still very good and not so different that it ruins the experience

I genuinely tried to enjoy Dark Souls II, but it feels like the awkward middle child of the series. The combat is clunky, enemy placement feels cheap instead of challenging, and half the difficulty comes from getting mobbed by ten enemies at once. The hitboxes are legendary for all the wrong reasons — getting stabbed from three feet away is apparently part of the experience.

The world design also lacks the interconnected magic that made the first game memorable. Areas feel randomly stitched together, and the elevator from a windmill to a lava castle still makes absolutely no sense. Boss fights are mostly forgettable, with way too many “big armored guy” encounters that blur together after a while.

And don’t even get me started on adaptability. Locking basic dodge functionality behind a stat is one of the worst design choices FromSoftware ever made. Instead of feeling rewarding, the game often feels unfair and frustrating for the sake of being frustrating.

It’s not completely terrible — there are some cool ideas and decent DLCs — but compared to the other Souls games, this one feels bloated, inconsistent, and unfinished.

 I have completed the game elsewhere 3 separate times to confirm i was not crazy and tried to play once again here on pc but got 4 hours deep and couldn't bear another trip through.

There's definitely something here but its not fully realized and feels like it was trying to be unique and succeeding for all the wrong reasons. Its uniquely buttcheeks.

All of this said Majula is easily one of the most beautiful areas in the series and I find myself revisiting from time to time for this reason.

My review time does not include the several hundred hours on console I played. 

Plays like you're trying to play an online fighting game with a full second of latency. In comparison to its direct predecessor and direct seuqel, Dark Souls 2 has squishy controls that don't act like youre expecting despite looking just similar enough on the surface. It doesn't feel good to play, and the hitboxes are notoriously jank. 

Don't listen to the apologists. This is a Souls-Like, not a Souls-Game. Souls games might *market* themselves on how hard they are, but they never actually follow through on make a rage game. DS2 did. It bought into the hype of PREPARE TO DIE EDITION and decided to make a game that plays like ass and is full of blatantly unfair fights. What else do you expect when you hand a new genre of game to a new team that has no overlap with the main team that built said genre? 

Just because it has the Fromsoft logo on it doesn't change that fact. If you're looking for a new souls experience because you've exhausted all the other games in the franchise, just replay one  you've beaten. It will be more fun. 

If you're about to play Lords of the Fallen or something, then yeah, I guess buy this first, but only in that niche case where you're so starved for souls content after putting 1000+ hours in DS1,3, Sekiro, and Elden Ring you're considering buying a Souls-Like.
clunky ganksquad joke of a souls game, i tried so hard to like it. the world is fantastic but the game mechanics are absolute garbage

It's the original Dark Souls 2 game with worse enemy placements. 
The Souls series is about so much more then just "being hard", but everyone complained about Dark Souls 2 being too easy. Now this version has absurd and frustrating enemy counts and powerful spells have been debuffed into oblivion. 

I'm starting to wonder if this game just has such a bad rep, because newcomers are playing this edition.
Dark Souls 2 is the most important game in the series, because it showed everything one shouldn't do to make a good souls game
This is without a doubt one of the worst games I have ever played. Balance is non-existent, enemy placement is insane, and the amount of projectile spam is baffling.
Don't listen to anyone about this game, if you loved Dark souls 1. You will love this, its different sure. That is a good thing my friend.

I have an abusive relationship with [b] Dark Souls II [/b]. 

[h1] Background [/h1] 
After the release and success of [b] Dark Souls [/b] in 2011, [b] Dark Souls II [/b] was announced shortly thereafter in 2012 and released in 2014. The game was directed by Tomohiro Shibuya, rather than Hidetaka Miyazaki, due to Miyazaki moving on to direct [b] Bloodborne [/b]. The game largely expands the gameplay of the original [b] Dark Souls [/b] while attempting to maintain the same challenge as the first offered. The game was released to astounding success. Although critics have stated that it falls short of [b] Dark Souls [/b]’ achievements.  

[b] Dark Souls II [/b] received a remaster in the form of [b] Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin [/b]. This remaster combined [b] Dark Souls II [/b] and its DLC’s into one package. In addition to this, the remaster changed the game itself. Enemy positions and behaviors were changed, performance improved, and the addition of a new character and boss. This is the only version of [b] Dark Souls II [/b] I have played. So, I’m not here to compare Vanilla and Scholar versions. Maybe that’s for when I actually play Vanilla. 

[h1] Story [/h1] 
The First Flame fades again, causing the undead curse to reappear. Seeking a cure to their undeath, the Bearer of the Curse seeks a passage to the far-off kingdom of Drangleic. Finding a vortex, the Bearer jumps in and arrives at the home of the Fire Keepers, women responsible for tending the bonfires that channel the First Flame. The Fire Keepers help the Bearer to remember some of their past and temporarily cure them of their undeath through the use of a human effigy. The Bearer is then directed to Majula, to seek stronger souls, and seek the King, lest they let the curse swallow them whole. 

[h1] Gameplay [/h1]
[b] Dark Souls II [/b] plays almost exactly like [b] Dark Souls [/b]. For those in the audience who have never played a [b] Souls [/b] game, let me elucidate. It’s an RPG, you start the game by selecting your class and working towards a specific build. Instead of experience points, you gain souls from defeating enemies. You use these souls to level up your character. However, if you die with these souls, you lose them. Don’t worry, there is a chance for retrieval if you return to where you died. But if you die before retrieving them, they’re gone for good. Combat consists of using melee weapons, magic, pyromancy, and more. A weapon can be equipped with either your left or right hand or even both. To defend yourself, there are shields that are equippable or the trusty dodge roll. While fighting, you can switch to two-hand either your left or right-handed weapon, as well. A notable change in combat from [b] Dark Souls II [/b] is power stance. Using the right weapons, when you try to two-hand your left weapon, the Bearer will instead use both weapons and do almost twice the damage. Pretty cool, huh? 

[b] Dark Souls II [/b] aims to keep the same challenge that people loved from [b] Dark Souls [/b]; and while I feel like it does that well in some areas, it does it very poorly in others. For instance, a lot of the bosses in the game are well designed to give you a tough time. Looking-Glass Knight, Smelter Demon, Fume Knight. All great fights that capture the challenge and make you feel accomplished for overcoming. But the levels themselves... ugh. Why am I fighting two Alonne knights, while two others shoot at me with bows? This is why [b] Dark Souls II [/b] is infamous, its extraordinary number of enemy groups. Now this isn’t a new concept, [b] Dark Souls [/b] often had you fight groups of enemies. In [b] Dark Souls II [/b] though, it feels like it’s everywhere. Most times when you enter a new area, at least two enemies will aggro. While you’re fighting these two, you’ll trigger another aggro. And while you have three enemies on you, there will be another one shooting you with arrows. The number of times where you just fight a single enemy is so seldom, it's unbelievable. It’s difficult not because it’s challenging, but because it’s frustrating. 

Now one thing I’ll give [b] Dark Souls II [/b] over all the other games in the series. It actually makes New Game Plus worth it. In most other [b] Souls [/b] games, New Game Plus is there to go through the journey again, but with your current stats and tougher enemies, minimal other changes. [b] Dark Souls II [/b] actually adds new enemies, changes some bosses up, and even adds new items. These changes make the second journey feel more worth it than it does in the other [b] Souls [/b] games. It’s cool to see what new weapons or spells you’ll be able to find. Plus, it's the only way to get my one true love, the Moonlight Greatsword. 

As with the rest of the series, multiplayer is a feature in [b] Dark Souls II [/b] as well. For cooperative play, the player can summon other players (or NPCs) for help with a boss or navigating an area. Or if you’re feeling devilish, the player can invade other people’s games and give them a hard time. Some people complain about the “Soul Memory” mechanic in this game, but I don’t think it’s that big of a deal. It helped for better matchmaking and preventing twinks. My biggest complaint is, and I’m not even sure if it’s valid because of internet connections, that the hit detection feels completely jank. I’ll be taking damage from hits that missed me by three feet. Someone will get a backstab on me from the front. Don’t even get me started on parrying; it isn’t even worth it because the timing will always be off. 

[h1] Gamefeel [/h1]
Despite all my grievances with [b] Dark Souls II [/b]’s gameplay, I must say that its atmosphere is unmatched. I love the land of Drangleic so much and the story that it tells. I care for maybe two or three NPC storylines in the rest of the series combined. But in [b] Dark Souls II [/b], all the NPCs are so charming and dedicated to their missions. I can’t help but get invested in their stories and grow fond of the characters. Each playthrough, I’ve failed in not wearing Lucatiel’s armor [spoiler] in her memory [/spoiler] after finishing her storyline. [b] Dark Souls II [/b] probably has the best NPC quests in the series. 

All the [b] Dark Souls [/b] games are known for their impressive views that will secretly foreshadow the coming areas. [b] Dark Souls II [/b] is no exception to this. I love worldbuilding like this. You’ll come across a view of a large castle in the distance and think to yourself, “Huh, neat.” Then later in the game, you’ll arrive at a castle, look down over a cliffside, and realize, “♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, that’s the whole path I took here. This is that same castle I saw earlier.” This is probably my favorite thing about all the [b] Dark Souls [/b] games. The game world feels so interconnected and [i] almost [/i] believable ( [i] cough [/i] Earthen Peak to Iron Keep [i] cough [/i]).  

[h1] Conclusion [/h1] 
People like to rag on [b] Dark Souls II [/b] a lot. I can’t necessarily blame them. There are good reasons to rag on this game, but also bad reasons. Despite everything though, I do really like [b] Dark Souls II [/b]. It has a charm to it that can’t be found, in my opinion, in the rest of the franchise. I really wish that some of the gameplay designs were better. This would probably be my favorite [b] Dark Souls [/b] game if it had the level design philosophy found in the other games.  

I could never recommend this game to be anyone’s first Souls-like. [b] Dark Souls [/b] or [b] Dark Souls III [/b] (or even [b] Elden Ring [/b] now) are much better introductions to the genre. But once you have played any of these games, I could not encourage a playthrough of [b] Dark Souls II [/b] enough.

Dark Souls II is by far the most disgusting Souls game I have ever played.
It goes wrong right from the combat system. The Adaptability stat is an absolute disaster. In the early game, your rolls are terrible — short distance and very few invincibility frames. For anyone used to the rolling-focused combat in Dark Souls 1 and 3, this feels like actual torture. Even when the game improves your movement speed, the awful hitboxes and terrible lock-on system completely cancel it out. You’re forced into this weird, uncomfortable mix of running, positioning, rolling, and locking on just to fight bosses. The combat has none of the smooth rhythm and flow that defines the Souls series.
The level design is equally disastrous. There are far too many pointless doors — some can be broken, some can’t, and many can’t even be opened early on. Visual guidance is poor and the overall level design feels confused and lazy. While some difficult areas in Souls games can still spark a desire to explore, many zones in Dark Souls II feel like they were designed purely to annoy and frustrate players. The foggy forest area (Shaded Woods) is a perfect example — it’s nothing but pure torment with no sense of discovery.
What disappointed me the most is the boss design and presentation. Almost none of the base game bosses left any lasting impression. For example, the Flexile Sentry (that two-sided/conjoined monster) appears near the Lost Sinner’s flooded area and is later directly reused as another boss inside the ship at No-man’s Wharf. The Dragonrider shows up once early on, then you have to fight two of them together as a boss in Drangleic Castle. The entire approach feels extremely lazy and low-effort. Boss presentations are weak, the music is forgettable, and the spectacle is nowhere near the level of Elden Ring, where even weaker bosses can still feel memorable thanks to music, art direction, and atmosphere.
I was so turned off by the base game that I didn’t even bother with the DLC. None of the base game bosses felt worth experiencing. Combined with the janky hitboxes, the combat and exploration experience is just consistently bad.
If I had to score it out of 10, I would only give Dark Souls II a 2/10. The only reason it gets those 2 points is because the dialogue and item descriptions actually have more content and depth than in Dark Souls 1 and 3 — they do a better job of telling the stories of the kingdoms and characters. But everything else — combat, level design, bosses, and overall presentation — is so disappointing and frustrating that it completely overshadows any positives.
